title_tesim: Jessie Ralph, actress, circa 1933-1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Jessie Ralph. Prints.

On back of photo: [Press tag:] Jessie Ralph, veteran stage actress, who was placed under a Columbia contract as a result of her excellent work in “Child of Manhattan,” in which she made her motion picture debut.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: ark:/21198/zz002jdk1t (back of photo)
Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Jessie Ralph and Richard Cromwell did not appear together on film. It appears that “Biddy” was not produced.
Jessie Ralph began as a theater actor, and transitioned into film work in the 1930’s when she was in her 70’s. She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures spanning the years 1933-1934.

title_tesim: Porter Hall drinking from a basketry dish on the set of Arizona, circa 1940
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Arizona.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Columbia Pictures production was filmed on location in Arizona.

Photograph of actor Porter Hall (who portrayed Lazarus Ward) on the set of the  western film Arizona (1940). Hall is photographed in costume - wearing a hat, overcoat, and vest with watch chain visible, and a gun belt around his waist (gun in holster) - stands next to a cactus as he drinks from a small Southwestern basketry dish with stepped design. Drops of water are seen trickling from the woven dish. A ramada (an open air shelter made of thatch and branches) stands directly next to the cactus, providing shade for the actor as well as a second man (wearing a short sleeved shirt and helmet imprinted with a red cross) seen in the background.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Mel Ferrer as matador Luís Bello in The Brave Bulls, 1951
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Brave Bulls 1951. Negatives Additional. Acetate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Brave Bulls was shot at Columbia Studios and on location in Mexico City, spring of 1950. The film was released in 1951.

A bullfighter - the matador - captured as he is being handed a blade by a "sword page" (mozo de espadas). The matador wears traditional attire, including a montera hat, short embroidered jacket (chaquetilla), and close-fitting pants. The photograph captures the matador just as he is throwing his cape a wooden barrier. In the background is an additional bullfighter holding a cape. Signs along the arena stands read: “Stetson,” “Nieto Regalos,” “Stetson,” Gayosso,” “Madero …” The arena is possibly the Plaza de Toros in Mexico City.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

Handwritten on negative: BK-P.20A
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Portrait photograph of actress Patricia Medina, in costume as Linet, from the Columbia Pictures production The Black Knight (1954). Medina wears a dress with lace-up bodice and hanging chiffon sleeves – a costume meant to evoke the Middle Ages. The finial, seen over her shoulder at right, is a representation of a lion holding a shield.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Francis Ryan and Grady Sutton, actor, ephemera, 1941
summary_tesim: Text from ephemera: Frank Ryan + Grady Sutton. ["They ..." crossed out] She Knew All the Answers.
subject_topic_tesim: She knew all the answers (Motion picture)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: According to the American Film Institute (AFI) catalog the filming of She Knew All the Answers took place from January 23, 1941 to March 3, 1941. The film was released on May 14, 1941.

On the negative enclosure and related ephemera, the man on the left is identified as Frank Ryan. The only person with a similar name related to She Knew All the Answers is Francis Ryan, who, according to the American Film Institute (AFI) catalog contributed dialogue to the film.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986
combined_subject_ssim: She knew all the answers (Motion picture)

title_tesim: Jessie Ralph, actress, caption on back of photo, circa 1933-1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Jessie Ralph. Prints.

Text on back of photo: [Typescript:] Jessie Ralph in an unusual character pose that of a drunken sot made by the noted camera artist Carl De Voy. Miss Ralph is one of Broadway’s popular actresses, who scored an immediate success with her performance in “Child of Manhattan.” She has been cast to co-star with Richard Cromwell in “Biddy,” a story of a mother’s love. [Stamped:] Please credit Carl De Voy, portrait artist, Columbia Studios.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: ark:/21198/zz002jdm0s (front of photo)
Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: It appears that “Biddy,” starring Jessie Ralph and Richard Cromwell, was not produced. The actors never appeared together on film.
Jessie Ralph began as a theater actor, and transitioned into film work in the 1930’s when she was in her 70’s. She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures spanning the years 1933-1934.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

Recruited from Broadway, Miss Ralph is sure to become one of the screen’s most beloved characters. She will next be seen in the Columbia production, “Biddy” with Richard Cromwell. title_tesim: Constance Towers, singer and actress, appearing on a marquee with other performers, Olympia Theater, Miami, 1954
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Connie Towers.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Olympia Theater and Office Building(174 East Flagler Street) is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

Singer and actress Constance "Connie" Towers’ stage and screen career spans six-decades and includes radio work, nightclub performances (supporting and headlining), starring roles in Broadway musicals, guest appearances on episodic television and roles in several television soap operas. She appeared in two Columbia Pictures productions: Bring Your Smile Along (1955) and Fast Forward (1985).

Constance "Connie" Towers appeared as a singer with actor/emcee Jack Carson, "aero-dancers" Ralph & Lorraine, and magician Viggo Jahn at the Olympia Theatre in Miami, March 30, 1954 (as noted in "House Review: Olympia, Miami," Variety, 31 Mar. 1954: 63).
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

She appeared in two Columbia Pictures productions: Bring Your Smile Along (1955) and Fast Forward (1985).
Prints possibly rephotographed circa 1955.

Image comprising four photographs of Constance Towers (b. 1933) and another young girl (likely Lucille Towers, listed with Constance in the 1940 U.S. census). Also included is a promotional announcement for a performance. Item reads: "The St. Regis Maisonette presents Connie Towers with Roy Chamberland at the piano, Milt Shaw's Orchestra and Ray Bari Ensemble. Theatre Dinner, Dinner, Supper." Towers' debut was noted in "Walter Winchell...of New York: Broadway Beacon," The Washington Post and Times Herald, 7 Apr. 1954: 51.

title_tesim: Audrey Totter, actress, with husband Dr. Leo Fred and two others on their wedding day, Village Lutheran Church of Westwood, 1952
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Assignment Paris. Acetate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Audrey Totter, wearing a wedding dress and holding a bouquet of flowers, standing arm-in-arm with her husband, Dr. Leo Fred, wearing a suit, tie and boutonniere. The couple are flanked by a man and women - likely the best man and maid (or matron) of honor. All were photographed at the Village Lutheran Church of Westwood.
A similar image, taken on the same occasion, appears as a photo standalone without a title in the Marriage Announcements section of the Los Angeles Times, 21 Sep. 1952: 3.
Audrey Totter famously portrayed the "bad girl" in numerous noir films. She appeared in the Columbia Pictures production, Assignment Paris (1952).
Related ephemera (image arc no. 21198/zz002jd3h9).
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Charles Belden, screenwriter, circa 1934
summary_tesim: Handwritten on negative: L-2.
Text from negative enclosure: Charles Belden.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Charles Belden was a screenwriter, though none of his scripts or stories were ever produced by Columbia. However, according to the Los Angeles Times article, “Greta Garbo Decides to Remain with Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer for Another Picture,” [2 Oct. 1934: 15], Columbia purchased “All His Geese Are Swans,” written by Belden and Frederick Stephant. The film does not seem to have been made.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: C. Bakaleinikoff, musical director, back of photo, circa 1933
summary_tesim: [Press tag:] Constantine [sic] Bakaleinikoff. “Background music” for many of the most important motion pictures produced in Hollywood is composed or arranged by Constantine [sic] Bakaleinikoff, who once directed the Imperial Orchestra in Moscow. He is now attached to the Columbia studios, and scored the currently successful “Cocktail Hour,” as well as the unreleased picture, “Lady for a Day.” Recently he was guest conductor of the Philharmonic Orchestra of Los Angeles, by courtesy of Columbia. [Stamp:] Please credit Carl De Voy, portrait artist, Columbia Studios.
Text from negative enclosure: Constantine [sic] Bakaleinikoff (music).

title_tesim: Joseph Schildkraut, actor, standing in a group raising a toast, circa 1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Joseph Schildkraut. Nitrate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Rabbi Edgar F. Magnin is the fourth person from the left. Joseph Schildkraut's mother, Erna, sits at the center of the image. Mr. Schildkraut's wife, Marie, sits behind her, and Mr. Schildkraut stands behind his wife wearing a button down shirt.

Joseph Schildkraut appeared in dozens of films from the 1920's through the 1960's and won the Oscar for Best Supporting Actor for his role in The Life of Emile Zola (1937). He appeared in one film produced by Columbia Pictures: Sisters Under the Skin (1934).

title_tesim: Tess Gilbert, script assistant, circa 1933
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Tess Gilbert [crossed out:] script. Portraits G.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Tess Gilbert does not appear in the AFI catalog. However, she refers to herself as a screen actress in "My Beauty Hint," The Reading Eagle, 22 Feb. 1933: 9; and she is identified, in the Brooklyn Daily Eagle 12 Apr. 1936: 43, as a "script girl" for the Capra film "Ladies of Leisure" (1930), produced by Columbia Pictures. She referred to herself as a script writer for the 1930 Census, which lists her place of birth as Russia.

description_tesim: Ralph Bellamy's decades-long film career began in the early 1930's. He appeared in many films produced by Columbia Pictures, the first of which was Forbidden in 1932

title_tesim: back of 21198/zz002jg1pt - Frank Capra, director, holding his Academy Award for Best Director for It Happened One Night, Los Angeles, verso of print, 1935
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Academy Awards.

[Press tag:] Frank Capra, Hollywood’s No. 1 director, receives the award from the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences for the outstanding directorial achievement of 1934. The Columbia picture, “It Happened One Night”, was also acclaimed the finest production of the year, and its stars, Claudette Colbert and Clark Gable, received awards for their acting. Columbia received eight out of eleven first places on this night of nights!

title_tesim: Lawrence Pohle (probably), writer, circa 1936-1937
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Negative misfiled in enclosure with Dore Schary

Lawrence Pohl was writer for movies and theater. He is credited as a contract writer for the movie Breakfast for Two (1937), a story writer for The Big Shot (1937) and Lets Make a Million (1936) (AFI catalog). He co-wrote three plays which were produced from 1934 to 1941: "Preface to Love" with Thomas Ahearn, "Alley Cat" with Alan Dinehart, and "Wise Girl" with Betty Laidlaw. He also co-wrote two screenplays with Thomas Ahearn that were not produced: "Let's be Candid," and "Take the Heir," the latter purchased by R.-K.-O.

title_tesim: Lawrence Pohle (probably), writer, circa 1936-1937
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Lawrence Poble [sic, probably "Pohle
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Lawrence Pohl was writer for movies and theater. He is credited as a contract writer for the movie Breakfast for Two (1937), a story writer for The Big Shot (1937) and Lets Make a Million (1936) (AFI catalog). He co-wrote three plays which were produced from 1934 to 1941: "Preface to Love" with Thomas Ahearn, "Alley Cat" with Alan Dinehart, and "Wise Girl" with Betty Laidlaw. He also co-wrote two screenplays with Thomas Ahearn that were not produced: "Let's be Candid," and "Take the Heir," the latter purchased by R.-K.-O.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

description_tesim: Nina Wilcox Putnam was an author who occasionally wrote for the movies. Though she did not write any Columbia Pictures films that were produced, according to the Los Angeles Times article, "Woes of Rich Will Be Told" [21 May 1932: A7], Ms. Putnam was hired by Columbia to write the screenplay for the adaptation of "No More Orchids."

repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Nina Wilcox Putnam was an author who occasionally wrote for the movies. Though she did not write any Columbia Pictures films that were produced, according to the Los Angeles Times article, "Woes of Rich Will Be Told" [21 May 1932: A7], Ms. Putnam was hired by Columbia to write the screenplay for the adaptation of "No More Orchids."
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Laurence Stallings, screenwriter, and Lewis Milestone, director, stepping off of a train, circa 1933
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Lewis Milestone (director) (with Laurence Stallings).
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Director Lewis Milestone and screenwriter Laurence Stallings did not collaborate on any Columbia Pictures films that were produced. However, according to the Los Angeles Times article, "True View of Russia to be Given in Film: Milestone, Stallings Back from Moscow Trip with Unique View," [10 Dec. 1933: B1], the two men were planning a film called "Red Square" to be produced by Columbia. The film was never made.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Non-Royal Self-Representation
summary_tesim: In ancient Egypt the primary intention of creating textual self-presentations—or self-portraiture in words, similar to that in paintings, statuary, and reliefs—was to present the explicit characteristics of protagonists in a corresponding fashion, introducing their values and effectiveness to live and rejoice in immortality, both in the afterlife and in the consciousness and thoughts of Egypt’s subsequent generations. The practice of self-presentation was rooted in Egyptian literature from at least the Third Dynasty, and through the course of dynastic history, it differed in aspect, composition, and theme. Self-presentations show the lives of the elites, vividly portraying their beliefs, culture, and expectations for the afterlife. The relationship between royalty and nobility in self-presentations is alluring and informative and compels us to envision the times and the contingencies in which they were created. These texts also make explicit their owners’ wish to be remembered—not forgotten—after death. The presentation of the non-royal self in ancient Egypt represents a window into its culture and historical periods.
member_of_collections_ssim: UCLA Encyclopedia of Egyptology (UEE)
